markdown:
## Prototyping


<definition term="Prototyping">Prototyping** **is the process of creating a model or early version of a product to explore ideas, test concepts, and gather feedback before final production.</definition>

1. Prototyping is a critical step in the **iterative design process**
2. Two primary techniques are used: 
  1. **low-fidelity** prototyping
  2. **high-fidelity** prototyping

![Four prototypes of the padlock design with varying fidelity (Jensen et al. 2018)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c262efa35639dc114a5751290144dfc4e17f0a26-983x624.png)

| Aspect | Low-Fidelity | High-Fidelity |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Detail Level | Basic, conceptual | Detailed, realistic |
| Cost and Time | Low | High |
| User Feedback | Focuses on core concepts | Provides accurate insights |
| Flexibility | Easy to modify | More challenging to change |
| Use Cases | Early-stage exploration | Final-stage testing and presentation |

### Low-Fidelity Prototyping


<definition term="Low-Fidelity Prototypes">Simple, quick, and inexpensive representations of a product. Often made using **paper, cardboard, or rough digital sketches**.</definition>

![Low-fidelity prototyping (D2M, 2019)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ee44ecc1114e1cc3073a1be810d8e45cc8e1a663-800x533.png)

<callout type="example">* **Cardboard model of a handheld vacuum **to test grip and reach
* **Foam block mock-up of a TV remote **to evaluate size and button placement
* **Paper and string prototype of a foldable chair **to test folding mechanics
* **Clay mock-up of a toothbrush handle **to trial the ergonomic shape
* **3D sketch model of a desk lamp **using simple materials to test proportions</callout>

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
| --- | --- |
| Fast and cheap to create | Limited interactivity and realism |
| Identifies major design flaws early, minimising costly changes later  | Doesn’t reflect real-world performance |
| Encourages early feedback and iteration | Users may struggle to envision the final product, affecting the quality of feedback. |
| Minimal resources or tools required | Inadequate for evaluating technical performance |

### High-Fidelity Prototyping


<definition term="High-Fidelity Prototypes">Detailed, realistic models that closely mimic the **look, feel, and functionality **of the final product. Can be **digital (interactive) or physical (functional)**.</definition>

![High-fidelity prototype of the Dyson Air-Purifying Headphones](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/a6a8842d4dec85b82ef1409dff2cd5a627f94e9c-1920x1440.png)

<callout type="example">* **3D-printed enclosure of a wearable fitness tracker **with working strap and buttons
* **Functional prototype of a kitchen appliance **with real electronics inside (e.g. blender or kettle)
* **Vacuum-formed plastic model of a computer mouse **with working scroll and click
* **High-detail foam or resin model of a hairdryer **with correct proportions, vents, and cable</callout>

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
| --- | --- |
| Allows realistic user testing | More time-consuming and expensive to make |
| Closely replicates final design | Users may focus on visuals over function |
| Helps test fine details (e.g. controls) | Can slow down iteration early in the process |
| Allow users to engage with the prototype as they would with the finished product | may become attached to specific details, making it harder to pivot if major changes are needed |
| Stakeholders take it more seriously, increasing buy-in |  |

### Choosing the Right Prototyping Technique


1. When deciding between low and high-fidelity prototyping, consider the specific goals of your design process. 
  1. **Start with low-fidelity **prototypes to explore ideas quickly and get user feedback
  2. **Progress to high-fidelity **as the design becomes more refined and ready for testing

<callout type="case_study">* James Dyson built over 5,000 prototypes when developing his first bagless vacuum. 
* Starting with simple cardboard models, he gradually moved to high-fidelity versions with working parts. 
* This iterative process helped refine performance, usability, and durability before final production.

![James Dyson prototyping](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/28841b0fd428ffa797f231456886918dbce65664-1280x720.png)</callout>

<example_question>
        <question>
        **Which of the following terms refers to the degree of realism of a model or simulation?**

  1. Instrumented
  2. Range of fidelity
  3. Haptic technology
  4. Finite element analysis
        </question>
        <answer>
        Award 1 mark for the correct answer: b - range of fidelity
        </answer>
      </example_question>

<callout type="self_review">Consider a recent prototype you have created:

* Would you deem it a low or high fidelity model?
* Did the model allow you to obtain the feedback or data you were seeking?</callout>
